name: Khepri
description: Khepri, also known as Khepera, is an Egyptian god associated with the scarab beetle (Scarabaeus sacer), dung beetles, renewal, and the rising sun. His name means 'to come into being' or 'to transform.' He represents the sun’s daily rebirth and the cycle of life and death. Khepri is often depicted as a scarab beetle, or as a human with a scarab beetle head, pushing the sun across the sky. This action mirrors the dung beetle's behavior of rolling dung into a ball, which Egyptians saw as a symbol of the sun being moved across the heavens. He is closely linked with the god Ra, representing the morning sun, while Ra represents the midday sun and Atum the evening sun. Khepri's role in the daily solar cycle made him a powerful symbol of resurrection and eternal life. In funerary texts, Khepri is invoked to ensure the deceased's rebirth into the afterlife. Amulets in the shape of scarabs were often placed on mummies to aid in this process. His association with the rising sun also connects him to creation myths, where he is sometimes depicted as self-created, emerging from the primordial waters of Nun. He embodies the concept of self-generation and the cyclical nature of existence, making him a significant deity in the Egyptian pantheon. He represents the hope of a new beginning each day.
subtitle: God of the Rising Sun & Renewal

keyMyths.title: Khepri and the Rising Sun
keyMyths.description: Khepri is believed to push the sun across the sky each morning, mirroring the dung beetle rolling its dung ball. This symbolizes the sun's rebirth and the beginning of a new day.

keyMyths.title: Khepri's Self-Creation
keyMyths.description: In some creation myths, Khepri is depicted as emerging from the primordial waters of Nun, creating himself. This emphasizes his association with self-generation and the beginning of existence.

keyMyths.title: Khepri and the Afterlife
keyMyths.description: Scarabs representing Khepri were placed on mummies to ensure the deceased's resurrection into the afterlife. This symbolized the hope of rebirth and eternal life for the dead.

sources.author: Richard H. Wilkinson
sources.work: Complete Gods and Goddesses of Ancient Egypt

sources.author: James P. Allen
sources.work: Genesis in Egypt: The Philosophy of Ancient Egyptian Creation Accounts

sources.author: E. A. Wallis Budge
